claude.text: Race condition: BuildIndex() writes to b.cache while holding cacheMu.Lock(), but TotalDocs() reads from b.cache without any lock protection. This violates Go's map safety rules since TotalDocs() is called from Prometheus metrics collection which can happen at any time, including while concurrent BuildIndex() calls are writing to the cache during initialization. Go maps are not safe for concurrent read/write access, and this can cause panics or data corruption.

qodo.text: Initialization is now performed eagerly in NewResourceServer and gRPC handlers no longer call Init. Need to validate that all construction paths go through NewResourceServer (no other code path can create a server without calling Init), and that startup contexts/timeouts won't prematurely cancel long-running initialization (index build / watcher setup), which would reintroduce the original 'context cancelled' failures but now at process startup.

BuildIndex no longer holds cacheMu for the whole function and now only locks around writing to cache. Need to confirm all other accesses to b.cache are protected consistently (including reads and any delete/replace paths). If there are concurrent readers, consider RWMutex and/or ensuring the index object is fully ready before publishing it into the cache to avoid races.

The integration test is now skipped for Postgres due to CI instability, which reduces coverage for a likely primary DB target. Consider fixing/flaking it behind a retry, isolating the Drone issue, or moving the scenario to a more reliable test harness so Postgres remains covered.

bugbot.text: Race condition in concurrent index builds: Moving the mutex lock from the start of BuildIndex to only around the cache write allows multiple goroutines to call BuildIndex for the same key concurrently, causing failures when both threads attempt to call bleve.New() on the same directory path for file-based indexes, and risking event loss when the cache is overwritten by a concurrent build

copilot.text: The trace context returned from tracer.Start() should be captured and used for subsequent operations to ensure proper trace propagation. Currently, the context is discarded which means child operations won't be properly linked to this span.

Moving the lock from the beginning to only protect the cache write at the end could introduce a race condition. If multiple goroutines call BuildIndex concurrently for the same key, they will all build separate indices and race to update the cache. The last one to acquire the lock will overwrite the others, wasting resources. Consider using a sync.Map with LoadOrStore or implementing per-key locking to ensure only one goroutine builds an index for a given key at a time.

bugbot.text: The close() method stops the commit thread before shutting down the queue pool. With q.shutdown(immediate=False), workers continue processing remaining items in queues during queue_pool.shutdown(). Any offsets completed during this draining phase are marked complete via complete_offset() but never committed to Kafka because the commit thread has already exited. This can cause duplicate message processing on consumer restart.

SimpleQueueProcessingStrategy.close() shuts down the queue_pool that is owned by ResultsStrategyFactory. After a Kafka partition rebalance, create_with_partitions() reuses the same factory and passes the now-shut-down pool to a new strategy. The new strategy would attempt to submit work to dead worker threads and closed queues, causing the consumer to fail or hang after any rebalance event.

The standard pattern requires re-checking the cache condition after acquiring the write lock to prevent this redundant work.

bugbot.text: The method `include_website_name` is missing the required `?` suffix. Active Model Serializers uses `include_#{attribute}?` convention to conditionally include attributes. Without the `?`, this method won't be called as a conditional, and `website_name` will always be serialized (potentially as `null` when no website is set) instead of being omitted when `website` is not present.

The `website_name` attribute bypasses trust level protection since it's not in the `untrusted_attributes` list, while the `website` attribute is protected. This means the user's website domain can be exposed even when `website` itself is intentionally hidden for privacy reasons.

The `website_name` method repeatedly performs the same expensive operations: `URI(website.to_s)` is parsed four times, `website_host.split('.')` is called three times, and `discourse_host.split('.')` is called three times. These values could be computed once and stored in local variables.

`website_name` is derived directly from `website` but is not treated as an untrusted attribute, so low-trust users whose `website` is intentionally hidden via `untrusted_attributes` / `restrict_user_fields?` will still leak their website through `website_name`. A TL0 user viewed anonymously can have their website exposed via this new field.
`website_name` should be subject to the same untrusted gating as `website` (by adding it to `untrusted_attributes` or checking `scope.restrict_user_fields?`).

The new `website_name` specs only cover the happy path for a regular user and do not verify that `website_name` remains hidden for TL0 users viewed anonymously. RSS/remote HTML can contain dangerous constructs like javascript: URLs, malformed attributes, data URIs, or unexpected tags. Server-side sanitization should be applied to contents before storing/rendering, and unsafe URL schemes in href/src should be validated/stripped.

SSRF vulnerability: TopicEmbed.import_remote fetches arbitrary URLs via open(url).read and Jobs::PollFeed fetches SiteSetting.feed_polling_url via open(...) without hardening. A malicious/compromised URL could target internal network resources or cause resource exhaustion.

Remote fetching uses open(url).read without explicit timeouts, size limits, redirect limits, or network allow/deny lists. This can enable SSRF to internal services, large response memory pressure, or long-hanging jobs. Should add strict timeouts, limit redirects, constrain IP ranges, and consider using a hardened HTTP client.

Clickjacking vulnerability: EmbedController sets X-Frame-Options to ALLOWALL and relies on request.referer host matching. Referer is not a reliable security boundary and can be absent/spoofed. Should use CSP frame-ancestors with explicit allowed origins and validate embedding origin robustly.

greptile.text: Webhook authentication is vulnerable to timing attacks. String comparison allows attackers to brute-force the secret by measuring response times. Use constant-time comparison or a crypto library's compare function.

Decryption will fail silently if the encryption key env var is not set (empty string fallback). This creates credentials with corrupt data instead of failing fast. Check that the key is configured before attempting decryption.

bugbot.text: The `isTeamAdminOrOwner` variable uses `&&` (AND) but the name and intent indicate `||` (OR). The `isTeamAdmin` function already returns truthy for both ADMIN and OWNER roles, so using `&&` with `isTeamOwner` means team admins who aren't owners are incorrectly denied permission to add guests

copilot.text: Direct mutation of props: The value prop is being mutated directly with 'value = value || []'. This is generally considered bad practice in React as it mutates the input parameter.

Inconsistent naming: The action id is 'add_members' but the feature is about adding guests, not members. This creates confusion with the data-testid in AddGuestsDialog which is also 'add_members'. Should use 'add_guests' for consistency.

Critical authorization bug: The logic uses AND (&&) when it should use OR (||). Currently, a user must be BOTH an admin AND an owner to pass this check, which is impossible since a user can only have one role. This means team admins who are not owners cannot add guests.

The isInvalidEmail error state persists even after the user modifies the email values. Once set to true, it remains visible until the dialog is closed or canceled. Should reset this error state when the user modifies the multiEmailValue.

Case sensitivity issue in email comparison: The newGuests array contains email addresses as provided by the user, while attendee.email comes from the database. If there's any case difference, the comparison will fail and new guests will receive AttendeeAddGuestsEmail instead of AttendeeScheduledEmail.

Case sensitivity issue in duplicate check: The comparison between guest and attendee.email is case-sensitive. This means if an attendee exists with email 'User@Example.com' and someone tries to add 'user@example.com', it will be treated as a different email and allowed through.
Using array index as React key is an anti-pattern, especially when items can be added or removed. When an item is removed, React may incorrectly match components to their state, leading to bugs.
Error swallowing without proper logging: The catch block only logs a generic message without logging the actual error details. This makes debugging difficult if email sending fails.

Case sensitivity bug: The blacklistedGuestEmails are converted to lowercase, but the guest emails being compared are not. This means the blacklist check will fail if a guest email has uppercase characters.

The validation logic does not filter out empty strings before checking. When the dialog starts with multiEmailValue set to [''], if a user clicks 'Add' immediately, it will attempt to validate an array containing an empty string.

Missing eventTypeMetadata parameter: The sendAddGuestsEmails function does not check eventTypeDisableHostEmail and eventTypeDisableAttendeeEmail flags like other similar email functions in this file. This means emails will be sent even if the event type has these email notifications disabled.
